title: Bank Statement Analysis for Dropshipping Businesses
meta: Dropshippers show high card revenue, heavy ad spend, and supplier pass-through. Learn how lenders analyze dropshipping bank statements and separate real margin from volume.
content:
Dropshipping: Revenue Looks Big, Margin Is Buried

Dropshipping businesses route customer orders to third-party suppliers who ship directly. The bank statement shows strong card revenue but enormous offsetting outflows for supplier fulfillment and advertising — making true margin hard to see at a glance.

How Dropshipping Money Moves

  • Processor settlements: Deposits from Shopify Payments, Stripe, PayPal, often net of reserves and chargebacks.
  • Supplier outflows: Payments to AliExpress, CJ, or domestic suppliers for each fulfilled order — pure pass-through cost.
  • Ad-spend outflows: Heavy daily charges to Meta, TikTok, and Google — often the single largest expense.
  • Chargeback and reserve holds: Processor reserves and disputes reduce net deposits.

Why Ad Spend and Chargebacks Define Risk

A dropshipper running $200K/month in sales might net only 10-20% after product cost and ad spend. High chargeback ratios are the key danger sign — they signal customer dissatisfaction and can trigger processor account freezes. Lenders should weight chargeback frequency and the stability of the ad-to-revenue ratio heavily.

What Lenders Focus On

  • Net margin after supplier and ad outflows
  • Chargeback and dispute frequency
  • Processor reserve holds and account stability
  • Revenue durability (trend, not a single viral month)

Bottom Line

Dropshipping can be fundable but carries real platform and chargeback risk. StatementScrub separates ad spend, supplier pass-through, and chargebacks so lenders see the true, durable margin.
